name: Element.Input

unions.name: Option
unions.comment: Add choices to your radio and select menus.

aliases.name: Button

aliases.name: Checkbox

aliases.name: Text
aliases.type: { onChange : Maybe (String -> msg), text : String, placeholder : Maybe (Placeholder msg), label : Label msg, notice : Maybe (Notice msg) }

values.name: button
values.comment:
A standard button.

The onPress message will be sent on either `onClick` or when the enter key has been pressed.

The message will fire exactly once per enter key press when the key is lifted.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Button msg -> Element msg

values.name: checkbox
values.comment: Your basic checkbox
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Checkbox msg -> Element msg

values.name: newPassword
values.comment:
A password input that allows the browser to autofill.

It's `newPassword` instead of just `password` because it gives the browser a hint on what type of password input it is.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Text msg -> Element msg

values.name: option
values.type: value -> Element msg -> Option value msg

values.name: placeholder
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Element msg -> Placeholder msg

values.name: radio
values.comment:
Input.radio
        [ padding 10
        , spacing 20
        ]
        { onChange = Just ChooseLunch
        , selected = Just model.lunch
        , label = Input.labelAbove (text "Lunch")
        , options =
            [ Input.styledChoice Burrito <|
                \selected ->
                    Element.row
                        [ spacing 5 ]
                        [ el None [] <|
                            if selected then
                                text ":D"
                            else
                                text ":("
                        , text "burrito"
                        ]
            , Input.option Taco (text "Taco!")
            , Input.option Gyro (text "Gyro")
            ]
        }
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Radio option msg -> Element msg

values.name: radioRow
values.comment: Same as radio, but displayed as a row
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Radio option msg -> Element msg

values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Select option msg -> Element msg

values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Text msg -> Element msg

name: Element.Area
comment:
# Area Annotations

This module is meant to make accessibility easy! They're sign posts that accessibility software like screen readers can use to navigate your app.

All you have to do is add them to elements in your app where you see fit.

Here's an example of annotating your navigation area:

    import Element.Area as Area

    myNavigation =
        Element.row [ Area.navigation ]
            [-- ..your navigation links
            ]

@docs mainContent, navigation, heading

@docs announce, announceUrgently, aside, footer

values.name: announce
values.comment: Screen readers will announce when changes to this element are made.

values.name: announceUrgently
values.comment: Screen readers will announce changes to this element and potentially interrupt any other announcement.

values.name: heading
values.comment:
This will mark an element as `h1`, `h2`, etc where possible. Though it's also smart enough to not conflict with existing nodes. So, this code link [ Area.heading 1 ] { url = "http://fruits.com" , label = text "Best site ever" } will generate

Best site ever

name: Element

aliases.name: Column
aliases.type: { header : Element msg, view : record -> Element msg }

aliases.name: Element
aliases.comment:
The basic building block of your layout. Here we create a

    import Background
    import Element

    view =
        Element.el [] (Element.text "Hello!")

aliases.name: FocusStyle
aliases.type: { borderColor : Maybe Color, backgroundColor : Maybe Color, shadow : Maybe { color : Color, offset : ( Int, Int ), blur : Int, size : Int } }

aliases.name: Table
aliases.type: { data : List records, columns : List (Column records msg) }

values.name: above
values.comment: `above` takes a `Bool` first so that you can easily toggle showing and hiding the element.

values.name: column
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> List (Element msg) -> Element msg

values.name: decorativeImage
values.comment: If an image is purely decorative, you can skip the caption.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> { src : String } -> Element msg

values.name: download
values.comment: A link to download a file.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Link msg -> Element msg

values.name: downloadAs
values.comment: A link to download a file, but you can specify the filename.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> { label : Element msg, filename : String, url : String } -> Element msg

values.name: el
values.comment:
The basic building block of your layout.

    import Color exposing (blue, darkBlue)
    import Element exposing (Element)
    import Element.Background as Background
    import Element.Border as Border

    myElement : Element msg
    myElement =
        Element.el
            [ Background.color blue
            , Border.color darkBlue
            ]
            (Element.text "You've made a stylish element!")
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Element msg -> Element msg

values.name: empty
values.comment: Nothing to see here!

values.name: fill
values.comment: Fill the available space. The available space will be split evenly between elements that have `width fill`.

values.name: fillPortion
values.comment:
Sometimes you may not want to split available space evenly. In this case you can use `fillPortion` to define which elements should have what portion of the available space.

So, two elements, one with `width (fillPortion 2)` and one with `width (fillPortion 3)`. The first would get 2 portions of the available space, while the second would get 3.

Also: `fill == fillPortion 1`

values.name: forceHover
values.comment:
Any `hover` styles, aka attributes with `mouseOver` in the name, will be always turned on.

This is useful for when you're targeting a platform that has no mouse, such as mobile.

values.name: image
values.comment: Both a source and a description are required for images. The description is used to describe the image to screen readers.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> { src : String, description : String } -> Element msg

values.name: layout
values.comment: This is your top level node where you can turn `Element` into `Html`.
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Element msg -> Html msg

values.name: layoutWith
values.type: { options : List Option } -> List (Attribute msg) -> Element msg -> Html msg

values.name: link
values.comment:
link []
        { url = "google.com"
        , label = text "My Link to Google"
        }
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Link msg -> Element msg

values.name: newTabLink
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Link msg -> Element msg

values.name: noHover
values.comment: Disable all `mouseOver` styles.

values.name: paddingEach
values.type: { bottom : Int, left : Int, right : Int, top : Int } -> Attribute msg

values.name: paddingXY
values.comment: Set horizontal and vertical padding.

values.name: paragraph
values.comment: A paragraph will layout all children as wrapped, inline elements. import Element import Element.Font as Font Element.paragraph [] [ text "lots of text ...." , el [ Font.bold ] (text "this is bold") , text "lots of text ...." ] This is really useful when you want to markup text by having some parts be bold, or some be links, or whatever you so desire. Also, if a child element has `alignLeft` or `alignRight`, then it will be moved to that side and the text will flow around it, (ah yes, `float` behavior). This makes it particularly easy to do something like a [dropped capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Initial). import Element import Element.Font as Font Element.paragraph [] [ el [ alignLeft , padding 5 , Font.lineHeight 1 ] (text "S") , text "lots of text ...." ] Which will look something like
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> List (Element msg) -> Element msg

values.name: pointer
values.comment: Set the cursor to the pointer hand.

values.name: row
values.comment: If you want a row of elements, use `row`!
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> List (Element msg) -> Element msg

values.name: spacingXY
values.comment:
In the majority of cases you'll just need to use `spacing`, which will work as intended.

However for some layouts, like `textColumn`, you may want to set a different spacing for the x axis compared to the y axis.

values.name: table
values.comment:
Show some tabular data.

Start with a list of records and specify how each column should be rendered.

So, if we have a list of `persons`:

    type alias Person =
        { firstName : String
        , lastName : String
        }

    persons : List Person
    persons =
        [ { firstName = "David"
          , lastName = "Bowie"
          }
        , { firstName = "Florence"
          , lastName = "Welch"
          }
        ]

We could render it using

    Element.table []
        { data = persons
        , columns =
            [ { header = Element.text "First Name"
              , view =
                    (\person ->
                        Element.text person.firstName
                    )
              }
            , { header = Element.text "Last Name"
              , view =
                     (\person ->
                        Element.text person.lastName
                     )
              }
            ]
        }
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> Table data msg -> Element msg

values.name: text
values.comment:
Create some plain text.

    text "Hello, you stylish developer!"

**Note** text does not wrap by default. In order to get text to wrap, check out `paragraph`!

values.name: textColumn
values.comment: Now that we have a paragraph, we need someway to attach a bunch of paragraph's together. To do that we can use a `textColumn`. The main difference between a `column` and a `textColumn` is that `textColumn` will flow the text around elements that have `alignRight` or `alignLeft`, just like we just saw with paragraph. In the following example, we have a `textColumn` where one child has `alignLeft`. Elment.textColumn [ spacing 10, padding 10 ] [ paragraph [] [ text "lots of text ...." ] , el [ alignLeft ] empty , paragraph [] [ text "lots of text ...." ] ] Which will result in something like:
values.type: List (Attribute msg) -> List (Element msg) -> Element msg

name: Element.Lazy
comment:
Same as `Html.lazy`. In case you're unfamiliar, here's a note from the `Html` library!

    Since all Elm functions are pure we have a guarantee that the same input
    will always result in the same output. This module gives us tools to be lazy
    about building `Html` that utilize this fact.

    Rather than immediately applying functions to their arguments, the `lazy`
    functions just bundle the function and arguments up for later. When diffing
    the old and new virtual DOM, it checks to see if all the arguments are equal
    by reference. If so, it skips calling the function!

    This is a really cheap test and often makes things a lot faster, but definitely
    benchmark to be sure!

@docs lazy

_Note:_ For now only `lazy` that covers one argument can be provided. In `0.19` this will change!

name: Element.Background
comment:
@docs color, mouseOverColor, gradient

@docs image, fittedImage, tiled, tiledX, tiledY

values.name: fittedImage
values.comment: Scale the image to fit the size of the element while maintaining proportions and cropping the overflow.

values.name: gradient
values.comment:
A linear gradient.

First you need to specify what direction the gradient is going by providing an angle in radians. `0` is up and `pi` is down.

The colors will be evenly spaced.

values.name: image
values.comment: A background image that keeps it's natural width and height.

values.name: mouseOverColor
values.comment: The background will change to this color when the mouse is over it.

values.name: tiled
values.comment: Tile an image in the x and y axes.

values.name: tiledX
values.comment: Tile an image in the x axis.

values.name: tiledY
values.comment: Tile an image in the y axis.

name: Element.Border

values.name: glow
values.comment: A simple glow by specifying the color and size.

values.name: innerShadow
values.type: { offset : ( Float, Float ), size : Float, blur : Float, color : Color } -> Attribute msg

values.name: roundEach
values.type: { topLeft : Int, topRight : Int, bottomLeft : Int, bottomRight : Int } -> Attribute msg

values.name: rounded
values.comment: Round all corners.

values.name: shadow
values.comment:
A drop shadow will add a shadow to whatever shape you give it.

So, if you apply a drop shadow to an image with an alpha channel, the shadow will appear around the edges of the non-trasparent part.
values.type: { offset : ( Float, Float ), blur : Float, color : Color } -> Attribute msg

values.name: widthEach
values.type: { bottom : Int, left : Int, right : Int, top : Int } -> Attribute msg

values.name: widthXY
values.comment: Set horizontal and vertical borders.

name: Element.Font

values.name: alignLeft
values.comment: Align the font to the left.

values.name: alignRight
values.comment: Align the font to the right.

values.name: center
values.comment: Center align the font.

values.name: family
values.comment:
import Element
    import Element.Font as Font

    myElement =
        Element.el
            [ Font.family
                [ Font.typeface "Helvetica"
                , Font.sansSerif
                ]
            ]
            (text "")

values.name: glow
values.comment: A glow is just a simplified shadow

values.name: letterSpacing
values.comment: In `px`.

values.name: lineHeight
values.comment:
This is the only unitless value in the library that isn't `px`.

It's given as a _proportion_ of the `Font.size`.

This means the final lineHeight in px is:

      Font.size * Font.lineHeight == lineHeightInPx

values.name: shadow
values.type: { offset : ( Float, Float ), blur : Float, color : Color } -> Attribute msg

values.name: size
values.comment: Font sizes are always given as `px`.

values.name: wordSpacing
values.comment: In `px`.
